items.title: v2.1.83
items.expandable_implement:
**To use transcript search:** Next time you're in a Claude Code session and need to find something from an earlier conversation, press `Ctrl+O` to enter transcript mode (you'll see your full conversation history). Press `/` and type your search term—maybe "haversine" or "GitHub Actions" or whatever you're looking for. Use `n` to jump to the next match, `N` to go back. Press `Esc` to exit search and return to transcript view. This is especially useful when you're building on something from a few days ago and don't want to re-explain the context.

**To reference pasted images:** When you're working on something that needs visual context (like generating a cleaning checklist from inspection photos), paste your images into Claude Code as usual. You'll now see `[Image #1]`, `[Image #2]`, etc. as chips in your input area. In your prompt, you can reference them directly: "Image 1 shows the bathroom counter. Image 2 shows the expected state. Write a Breezeway task that describes the gap between them." This makes multi-image prompts much clearer than saying "the first image" or "the one on the left."

The other features are either behind-the-scenes improvements or enterprise-focused (policy management, credential scrubbing). You'll benefit from them passively—faster startup, fewer weird hangs—but you don't need to configure anything.

items.title: Auto mode for Claude Code
items.expandable_implement:
To start using auto mode, update Claude Code to the latest version and run:

claude auto-mode enable

That's it — you're now using the classifier. To see exactly what rules are protecting you, run:

claude auto-mode defaults > auto-mode-rules.json

Open that file and scan through the 'allow' and 'block' sections. You'll see things like 'allow local file operations within project scope' but 'block operations in ~/ or /etc'. If you have a specific workflow that gets blocked — say, your GitHub Actions tool needs to read a config file from ~/.config — you can add custom rules to allow it. The key insight: the classifier only sees the current conversation context and the action Claude wants to take. If Claude asks to delete a file and you never mentioned deleting anything, the classifier blocks it. If you explicitly said 'delete all test files', the classifier allows it because it matches your intent. This is a middle ground between 'ask me every time' and 'trust blindly'.

items.title: Package Managers Need to Cool Down
items.expandable_implement:
If you're using uv (which you are for at least some projects), open your pyproject.toml and add:

[tool.uv]
exclude-newer = "72h"

That tells uv to only consider packages that were released at least 72 hours ago. If you have a trusted package that you want to update immediately (say, anthropic's official SDK), add:

exclude-newer-package = { anthropic = "0h" }

For pip, it's a bit more manual. You can use --uploaded-prior-to with an absolute timestamp, but that's annoying to maintain. Seth Larson's workaround (mentioned in the article) is to run a cron job that updates the timestamp in your pip.conf every day. Honestly, just switch those projects to uv — it handles relative durations natively and you're already using it elsewhere.

For GitHub Actions workflows, add this to your workflow file before any pip install step:

pip install uv
uv pip install --exclude-newer 72h -r requirements.txt

Now your CI pipeline has the same protection as your local environment. The key is consistency: if you protect local but not CI, an attacker just has to wait for your next deploy.

items.title: LiteLLM Hack: Were You One of the 47,000?
items.expandable_implement:
Audit your existing projects' dependency pinning:

1. For each project, check your requirements.txt or pyproject.toml. Look for entries like `litellm>=1.0.0` or `openai^1.0.0` — these would auto-upgrade.

2. Switch to exact pinning for critical dependencies: `anthropic==0.18.1` instead of `anthropic>=0.18.0`. This prevents automatic upgrades to compromised versions.

3. Use a tool like `pip-audit` to check your current dependencies for known vulnerabilities:
   ```bash
   pip install pip-audit
   pip-audit
   ```

4. For your GitHub Actions workflows, add a scheduled job that runs pip-audit weekly and opens an issue if vulnerabilities are found. This gives you a heads-up without blocking your daily work.

5. Consider adding a manual review step before upgrading any package that touches API credentials or processes external data. This is where Mario's 'slow down' advice applies — not to code generation, but to dependency updates that could compromise your entire system.

items.title: We Rewrote JSONata with AI in a Day, Saved $500K/Year
items.expandable_implement:
To try this pattern on a smaller scale:

1. Pick a slow or clunky component in your current stack — maybe a Python script that processes data slowly, or a module that would be simpler in a different language.

2. Write a comprehensive test suite first if you don't have one. Export sample inputs and outputs, edge cases, error conditions. The tests are what make this whole approach work.

3. Feed the tests and the original code to Claude and ask it to rewrite in your target language (Go for speed, TypeScript for web integration, whatever fits). Let it iterate against the test failures.

4. Once tests pass, run both versions in parallel on real data for a few days. Log any discrepancies. This is your safety net.

5. Only after confirming identical behavior do you switch over. This de-risks the migration completely.

Start small — a single utility function or data transformer. Build confidence before attempting something like the entire inspector scheduler.

items.title: Vibe coding SwiftUI apps is a lot of fun
items.expandable_implement: Start with a single-file approach like Simon did. Create a project directory and ask Claude to write a complete SwiftUI app in one file that does one specific thing you've been wanting—maybe a menu bar app that shows you today's STR occupancy across properties, or a simple window that monitors your GitHub Actions minutes usage. The key moves: (1) Ask Claude to suggest features rather than trying to spec everything upfront—it knows what's possible in SwiftUI better than you do. (2) Use git from the start so you can roll back when experiments don't work. (3) Keep the scope tiny initially—get something on screen that does the core thing, then iterate. You don't need to know Swift or SwiftUI; you're having a conversation about what you want to see and letting the LLM handle the implementation details. The full transcript Simon shared shows this is genuinely achievable even if you've never written a line of Swift.

items.title: Python Vulnerability Lookup
items.expandable_implement: Add a security check to your GitHub Actions workflows. In your .github/workflows directory, create a new job that runs after your tests. Install `requests` and write a short Python script that reads your requirements.txt, queries the OSV API for each package, and fails the build if vulnerabilities are found. Start simple: `import requests; response = requests.post('https://api.osv.dev/v1/query', json={'package': {'name': package_name, 'ecosystem': 'PyPI'}})`. If response.json().get('vulns'), print them and exit 1. This takes about 10 minutes to set up and runs automatically on every push. You can make it fancier later — ignore specific CVEs, set severity thresholds — but the basic version gives you visibility immediately.

items.title: Claude Code Changelog
items.expandable_implement: Start using --resume more aggressively when debugging or iterating on a problem. When you're working through a traceback or refining a feature, instead of starting a new session every time you hit a snag, use `claude code --resume` to pick up where you left off. This preserves context and lets Claude build on what it already learned about your codebase and your specific problem. The fix means this should now be reliable even on sessions you started weeks ago. Try it next time you're debugging the inspector scheduler or working on a new briefing feature — you'll save time not re-explaining the problem and the context you've already established.
